CG Weather Alert: तेज बारिश और 60 किमी की रफ्तार से चलेंगी हवाएं, कई जिलों के लिए चेतावनी
सार
छत्तीसगढ़ में दक्षिण-पश्चिम मानसून पूरी तरह सक्रिय है। लगातार हो रही बारिश से प्रदेश के कई इलाकों में नदी-नाले उफान पर हैं, जबकि निचले क्षेत्रों में जलभराव की स्थिति बनने लगी है।
विज्ञापन
खबरें लगातार पढ़ने के लिए अमर उजाला एप डाउनलोड करें
या
वेबसाइट पर पढ़ना जारी रखने के लिए वीडियो विज्ञापन देखें
अगर आपके पास प्रीमियम मेंबरशिप है तो
विस्तार
छत्तीसगढ़ में दक्षिण-पश्चिम मानसून पूरी तरह सक्रिय है। लगातार हो रही बारिश से प्रदेश के कई इलाकों में नदी-नाले उफान पर हैं, जबकि निचले क्षेत्रों में जलभराव की स्थिति बनने लगी है। मौसम विभाग ने अगले पांच दिनों तक प्रदेशभर में मानसूनी गतिविधियां तेज बने रहने का पूर्वानुमान जारी किया है। इसके मद्देनजर कई जिलों में ऑरेंज और यलो अलर्ट घोषित किया गया है।
राजधानी रायपुर में बुधवार सुबह से रुक-रुककर बारिश का सिलसिला जारी है। बादलों की वजह से तापमान में गिरावट दर्ज की गई है, जिससे लोगों को गर्मी से राहत मिली है। हालांकि कई इलाकों में जलभराव के कारण लोगों को आवागमन में दिक्कतों का सामना करना पड़ रहा है। मौसम विभाग के अनुसार रायपुर में दिनभर बादल छाए रहने के साथ गरज-चमक और बारिश की संभावना बनी रहेगी। शहर का अधिकतम तापमान 29 डिग्री और न्यूनतम तापमान 25 डिग्री सेल्सियस के आसपास रहने का अनुमान है।
प्रदेश के उत्तरी और मध्य हिस्सों में बारिश का असर अधिक देखने को मिला है। बीते 24 घंटे में बिलासपुर संभाग के अधिकांश क्षेत्रों में अच्छी बारिश दर्ज की गई, जबकि अंबिकापुर में अधिकतम तापमान 29.8 डिग्री सेल्सियस रिकॉर्ड किया गया। धमतरी जिले में लगातार बारिश से जनजीवन प्रभावित हो गया है। बांधों और जलाशयों का जलस्तर तेजी से बढ़ रहा है। कई स्थानों पर पानी भरने की स्थिति बनने के बाद प्रशासन ने संभावित बाढ़ जैसी परिस्थितियों से निपटने के लिए सतर्कता बढ़ा दी है।
विज्ञापन
बारिश के आंकड़ों पर नजर डालें तो रायगढ़ में सबसे अधिक 13 सेंटीमीटर वर्षा रिकॉर्ड की गई। इसके अलावा पेंड्रा में 12 सेंटीमीटर, पुसौर और तमनार में 10-10 सेंटीमीटर, बरपाली में 9 सेंटीमीटर तथा सारंगढ़, रामचंद्रपुर, मालखरौदा, अकलतरा, बलौदा और धाभरा सहित कई इलाकों में 7 सेंटीमीटर तक बारिश दर्ज की गई। प्रदेश के अनेक अन्य जिलों में भी 3 से 6 सेंटीमीटर तक वर्षा हुई।
मौसम विभाग के अनुसार प्रदेश में सक्रिय मानसून द्रोणिका, मध्यप्रदेश के ऊपर बने चक्रवाती परिसंचरण, बंगाल की खाड़ी में सक्रिय ऊपरी हवा के सिस्टम तथा छत्तीसगढ़ से गुजर रही द्रोणिका के संयुक्त प्रभाव से अगले 24 घंटों के दौरान अधिकांश क्षेत्रों में बारिश जारी रहने की संभावना है। कुछ स्थानों पर भारी वर्षा के साथ आकाशीय बिजली गिरने की भी आशंका जताई गई है।
इसी को देखते हुए बीजापुर, दंतेवाड़ा, बस्तर, नारायणपुर, कोंडागांव, कांकेर, धमतरी, बालोद, राजनांदगांव, गरियाबंद, रायपुर, बलौदाबाजार, जांजगीर-चांपा, रायगढ़, बिलासपुर, कोरबा, जशपुर, गौरेला-पेंड्रा-मरवाही, दुर्ग, बेमेतरा, सरगुजा, सूरजपुर, कोरिया और बलरामपुर जिलों के लिए तेज हवा और भारी बारिश को लेकर ऑरेंज अलर्ट जारी किया गया है। वहीं कई अन्य जिलों में सामान्य से मध्यम बारिश की संभावना को देखते हुए यलो अलर्ट भी प्रभावी रहेगा।
विज्ञापन

title: जमीन के साथ गई आमदनी लेकिन नहीं मिला मुआवजा, 8 साल बाद सड़क पर उतरे प्रभावित किसान - ETV Bharat
search_term: ("हीट एक्शन प्लान" OR "गर्मी से राहत" OR चेतावनी OR "रेड अलर्ट" OR "ऑरेंज अलर्ट" OR "येलो अलर्ट" OR एडवाइजरी OR "राहत सामग्री" OR "राहत शिविर" OR मुआवजा) Chhattisgarh
full_text:
जमीन के साथ गई आमदनी लेकिन नहीं मिला मुआवजा, 8 साल बाद सड़क पर उतरे प्रभावित किसान
कोरिया में सड़क निर्माण के लिए अधिग्रहित की गई भूमि का मुआवजा 8 साल बाद भी किसानों को नहीं मिला है.जिससे किसान आंदोलनरत हैं.
कोरिया : नेशनल हाईवे निर्माण के लिए अधिग्रहित भूमि का मुआवजा वर्षों बाद भी नहीं मिलने से प्रभावित किसानों और भू मालिकों का सब्र आखिरकार टूट गया. बैकुंठपुर से सूरजपुर के बीच राष्ट्रीय राजमार्ग निर्माण के दौरान वर्ष 2018 में पुल से लगी भूमि का अधिग्रहण किया गया था, लेकिन आठ साल बीत जाने के बाद भी 11 भूमि मालिकों को मुआवजे की राशि का भुगतान नहीं हो सका है. इससे नाराज प्रभावित परिवारों ने बैकुंठपुर में धरना-प्रदर्शन कर प्रशासन के खिलाफ जमकर नाराजगी जताई और जल्द भुगतान की मांग उठाई.
8 साल से मुआवजा का इंतजार
धरना-प्रदर्शन के दौरान बड़ी संख्या में प्रभावित भूमि मालिक मौजूद रहे.प्रदर्शन को देखते हुए किसी भी अप्रिय स्थिति से निपटने के लिए पुलिस बल भी मौके पर तैनात रहा.प्रदर्शनकारियों ने कहा कि उन्होंने अपनी जमीन राष्ट्रीय हित में सड़क निर्माण के लिए दी, लेकिन बदले में मिलने वाला मुआवजा आज तक नहीं मिला. कई बार अधिकारियों के चक्कर लगाने और आवेदन देने के बावजूद समस्या का समाधान नहीं हो सका.
मुआवजे की राशि नहीं मिलने से उनके सामने गंभीर आर्थिक संकट खड़ा हो गया है. कई परिवार खेती योग्य भूमि खो चुके हैं और अब आजीविका चलाने में कठिनाइयों का सामना कर रहे हैं. बच्चों की शिक्षा, पारिवारिक जिम्मेदारियां और कृषि संबंधी जरूरतें भी प्रभावित हो रही हैं- सुदीप गुप्ता, प्रभावित
प्रशासन पर लगाए गंभीर आरोप
ग्रामीणों ने प्रशासन पर मामले में लापरवाही बरतने का आरोप लगाते हुए कहा कि यदि समय रहते मुआवजे का भुगतान नहीं किया गया तो आंदोलन को और तेज किया जाएगा.क्योंकि 2018 में जमीन लिया गया था.इसके बाद मुआवजा राशि भी आ गई है.लेकिन स्थानीय प्रशासन मुआवजा राशि आठ साल से जारी नहीं कर रहा है. धरना स्थल पर मौजूद पूर्व सरपंच ने ब्याज के साथ राशि देने की मांग की है.
यह मामला लंबे समय से लंबित है और संबंधित विभाग को तत्काल हस्तक्षेप कर सभी पात्र भूमि मालिकों को उनका बकाया मुआवजा दिलाना चाहिए.हम चाहते हैं कि मुआवजा आने के बाद भी नहीं दिया गया.इसलिए जिन अधिकारियों ने मुआवजा रोका है,उनसे ब्याज वसूलकर राशि का भुगतान किया जाए- -राकेश सिंह,पूर्व सरपंच
ग्रामीणों ने दी उग्र आंदोलन की चेतावनी
ग्रामीणों का कहना है कि वर्षों से भुगतान का इंतजार करते-करते परिवार आर्थिक रूप से टूट चुका है. प्रशासन शीघ्र कार्रवाई करते हुए केवल आश्वासन नहीं, बल्कि भुगतान दे. प्रदर्शनकारियों ने चेतावनी दी कि यदि जल्द ही मुआवजे का भुगतान नहीं किया गया तो वे आंदोलन को और व्यापक रूप देंगे, जिसकी पूरी जिम्मेदारी प्रशासन की होगी.

title: Cooling systems becoming standard, as extreme heat becomes the norm - Pig World
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Chhattisgarh
full_text:
Nozzles installed above side inlets direct the mist into the incoming air, cooling it, while hot air is extracted by fans in the roof ridge
Large-scale pig farmers and integrators are now installing misting systems to cool their farrowing houses as standard, in response to the record temperatures caused by climate change, according to ARM Buildings.
Most systems involve misting water at high pressure with nozzles directing the vapour into the incoming air. “Misting systems are easy to fit and while I have had many calls from farmers without them, there have been very few issues of heat stress on farms where they are already in operation,” said Tim Miller, environmental specialist with ARM Buildings.
“From £100 per sow for a small house to as little as £40 per sow for a large multi-room building, it’s a no-brainer, considering the welfare and productivity benefits the cooling system brings in hot weather,” says
Explaining how they work, he said the mist is too fine to wet the house but reduces the temperature as it evaporates.
“I recently visited a farrowing house when the ambient temperature was 32⁰C but the room temperature was 28⁰C,“ he said. “Due to the ease of fitting, farmers can install the equipment themselves, in many cases using the existing Dicam controls to operate the system.”
He pointed out that if misting is used only a few times a year, then the huge welfare benefits far outweigh the initial cost. Any productivity improvements – such as better weaning weights resulting from sows retaining their appetites – are a bonus.
Farmers without cooling systems can mitigate problems by wetting the fabric of the building, especially concrete passageways, and upping the ventilation. “Some farmers use their pressure washers in front of the inlets to cool the incoming air but, obviously, care needs to be taken to avoid fans and other electrical equipment,” said Mr Miller.
Editor Pig World and the Tribune. National Pig Association webmaster. Former political editor at Farmers Guardian. Occasional media pundit. Brought up on a Leicestershire farm. Works from a shed in Oxfordshire garden.

title: Doctors warn GLP-1 users to watch for dehydration symptoms during First Alert Weather Days - WBRC
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Chhattisgarh
full_text:
Doctors warn GLP-1 users to watch for dehydration symptoms during First Alert Weather Days
Copyright 2026 WBRC. All rights reserved.
Skip to contentDoctors warn GLP-1 users to watch for dehydration symptoms during First Alert Weather Days
BIRMINGHAM, Ala. (WBRC) - As temperatures continue to climb this week during First Alert Weather Days, doctors are urging people to take heat safety seriously. Especially those folks taking popular GLP-1 medications prescribed for weight loss and diabetes.
Dr. Kre Johnson with Brownstone Healthcare said side effects tied to GLP-1s can increase the risk of dehydration in hot weather.
“That could cause a whole world of symptoms. That could be dizziness, lightheadedness, that could be fainting,” Johnson said.
Johnson said people taking GLP-1s should focus on hydration and replacing fluids they may lose through side effects, while also accounting for sweating in the summer heat.
“If you are on a GLP-1, you may want to try to get to 100 ounces of water today because you run the risk of some diarrhea - that’s a side effect. Vomiting and nausea are side effects,” Johnson said. “So you need to replenish that fluid you are losing, and then you are also sweating because here we are in the heat of the summer and we are in the South, so you have to be mindful.”
She adds that even people who are not taking a GLP-1 should pay close attention to how they feel in extreme heat, particularly if they take any type of medication. Johnson recommends planning and keeping water nearby so hydration does not become an afterthought.
“Prepare for the day. Go ahead and grab you some bottles of water, have them handy, so when you are out and about you already have it, and you don’t have to think about it,” Johnson said. “Try to drink 3 to 4 bottles of water in a day. We are sweating, we are losing our electrolytes, we are losing the liquids, and it is super hot out there. You want to be well because you don’t want to end up in the ER to end up rehydrated through IV fluids.”
Doctors say if you start feeling dizzy, lightheaded, or sick in the heat, get to a cooler place and start hydrating. If you cannot keep fluids down, feel confused, or feel like you might pass out, seek medical help right away.

Heavy Rain to Continue Across Odisha as IMD Issues Orange Alert for Five Districts
Bhubaneswar:heavy rainfall continued across several parts of Odisha on Wednesday as a low-pressure system remained active over the region, prompting the India Meteorological Department (IMD) to issue an Orange Warning for five districts and a Yellow Warning for 17 others.
According to the IMD, the current spell of monsoon rain, which began during the Rath Yatra period, is expected to persist for at least two more days, raising the possibility of heavy rainfall, thunderstorms and lightning in several parts of the state.
The IMD has issued an Orange Warning for Sundargarh, Jharsuguda, Sambalpur, Bargarh and Nuapada, where isolated places are likely to receive heavy to very heavy rainfall. Thunderstorms accompanied by lightning are also expected in these districts.
A Yellow Warning has been issued for Deogarh, Sonepur, Balangir, Boudh, Angul, Dhenkanal, Keonjhar, Mayurbhanj, Balasore, Bhadrak, Jajpur, Cuttack, Kendrapara, Jagatsinghpur, Kandhamal, Kalahandi and Nabarangpur.
These districts are likely to experience thunderstorms with lightning and heavy rainfall at isolated locations.
The weather department has advised residents to remain alert during thunderstorms and avoid taking shelter under trees or entering waterlogged areas during periods of intense rainfall and lightning activity.
With the low-pressure system expected to remain active, district administrations have been asked to stay prepared for possible localised flooding, waterlogging and rain-related disruptions, particularly in districts under Orange and Yellow warnings.
The IMD has urged people to monitor official weather updates and exercise caution while travelling over the next two days as monsoon activity continues across Odisha.

title: Mumbai under yellow alert today: Tansa Lake overflows, water stock in 7 reservoirs rises to 61.9% - CNBC TV18

full_text:
3 Min Read
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has placed Mumbai under a yellow alert until July 23, forecasting more spells of rain over the coming days. Amid the ongoing showers, Tansa Lake, one of the seven reservoirs supplying drinking water to Mumbai, started overflowing on Wednesday morning.
According to Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation (BMC), the lake started overflowing at around 8.51 am on Wednesday, prompting the opening of the dam gates. The lake, which has a storage capacity of 1,45,080 million litres (14,508 crore litres), is the second reservoir to reach full capacity this monsoon after Tulsi Lake.
As per the civic body, the combined water stock
On the same day last year, the seven reservoirs were 85.32% full, compared with 47.29% on July 22, 2024.
Among the reservoir catchments, Upper Vaitarna recorded the highest rainfall at 224 mm in the last 24 hours, followed by Modak Sagar (67 mm), Tansa (70 mm), Middle Vaitarna (71 mm), Bhatsa (96 mm), Vihar (65 mm) and Tulsi (71 mm).
Mumbai receives its drinking water from Tulsi, Vihar, Bhatsa, Tansa, Upper Vaitarna, Middle Vaitarna and Modak Sagar. While Tulsi and Vihar are located within the city, the remaining reservoirs are situated in Thane, Palghar and Nashik districts.
The BMC currently supplies around 3,850 million litres per day (MLD) of water against a daily demand of 4,200 MLD. At the present level, the available water stock is estimated to last 375 days.
Mumbai weather forecast
The IMD has issued a yellow alert in Mumbai on Wednesday, forecasting a generally cloudy sky with heavy rainfall very likely at isolated places. Occasional gusty winds reaching 50-60 kmph are also likely, with maximum and minimum temperatures expected to hover around 29°C and 25°C, respectively.

title: Pune Ghats Red Alert: Extremely Heavy Rainfall Expected - The Chenab Times
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Chhattisgarh
full_text:
Pune and its surrounding ghat regions are on high alert as the India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a red warning for Wednesday, July 22, forecasting extremely heavy rainfall. This alert signifies the highest level of weather warning, urging authorities and residents to “Take Action” due to the potential for hazardous weather conditions that could disrupt normal life.
Intense Rainfall Expected Across Ghats
The IMD’s red alert, valid from 8:30 am on July 22 to 8:30 am on July 23, specifically targets the ghat sections of Pune district. Authorities anticipate heavy to very heavy rainfall in several ghat areas, with the possibility of extremely heavy downpours in isolated locations. This intensified monsoon activity is attributed to a trough or low-pressure line extending from a cyclonic circulation over the West Central Bay of Bengal and adjoining north Coastal Andhra Pradesh to the North-East Arabian Sea. This system is enhancing moisture-laden winds from the Arabian Sea towards Maharashtra, contributing to the vigorous monsoon conditions.

Tamhini, a region known for receiving significant rainfall, recorded 275 mm, while other ghat stations like Bhira reported 309 mm. Lonavala experienced 142 mm, Shirgaon 255 mm, and Dawdi 264 mm, among other substantial amounts, highlighting the widespread heavy rainfall across the ghats.
The IMD has also issued orange alerts for the ghats of Nashik, Satara, and Kolhapur, indicating a similar pattern of heavy to very heavy rainfall in these neighbouring regions. The forecast suggests that these intense conditions might persist for the next 48 hours, with the alert levels expected to ease gradually after July 22.

The intense rainfall in the ghats poses several risks, including flash floods, landslides, mudslides, and rockfalls, especially in hilly and vulnerable areas. Reduced visibility on roads, slippery conditions, and temporary inundation in low-lying areas are anticipated, potentially causing significant disruptions to traffic and daily commutes. The IMD has advised residents and travelers to exercise extreme caution, monitor official weather updates, and avoid unnecessary travel to affected areas, particularly the ghat sections.
While the ghats are facing a red alert, Pune city itself is expected to receive light to moderate showers, mainly during the afternoon, with a slight drop in temperatures over the next two days. However, authorities are prepared for potential disruptions, including traffic delays and public transport interruptions. Residents are advised to stay indoors and follow safety guidelines during the period of intense showers.
In light of the weather warnings, authorities have cautioned against visiting ghat sections due to the likelihood of heavy rainfall and potential hazards. This advisory extends for the next 48 to 72 hours. The active monsoon conditions are expected to continue across Madhya Maharashtra, with similar weather patterns anticipated in surrounding regions like Konkan and Goa, which are under orange alerts for isolated very heavy rain.
The current spell of heavy rainfall is part of a broader monsoon surge affecting Western India. Meteorologists have noted that atmospheric conditions, including the formation of an upper-air cyclonic circulation over the Bay of Bengal, are contributing to the intensification of the monsoon over Maharashtra and neighbouring states. Rainfall is predicted to remain intense through the week, with some projections suggesting a potential easing of rainfall after July 26, depending on the movement of weather systems.

title: India Issues Red Alert for Himachal Pradesh and Warns Delhi Travelers as Heavy Monsoon Rainfall Triggers Landslide Risks: New Travel Alert - Nomad Lawyer
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Chhattisgarh
full_text:
Published on July 21, 2026
Extensive travel disruptions are impacting northern India as the southwest monsoon intensifies, prompting national weather authorities to issue urgent warnings. The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a red alert for the mountainous state of Himachal Pradesh, warning of extreme risks from persistent downpours. Meanwhile, the national capital of Delhi faces a yellow alert, where moderate showers have provided relief from the summer heat but caused localized street flooding. Travelers are urged to monitor real-time weather reports and postpone non-essential journeys to elevated terrains until conditions stabilize.
Quick Summary
- Himachal Pradesh Red Alert: The IMD has hoisted a red alert for the state, indicating severe hazard risks from heavy precipitation.
- Delhi Yellow Alert: The national capital remains under a yellow alert, experiencing moderate showers and localized waterlogging.
- Severe Environmental Hazards: Vulnerable sectors in the hills face high risks of sudden landslides, rockfalls, and flash floods.
- Transit Infrastructure Impacts: Debris accumulation has blocked vital mountain passes, while urban traffic in Delhi faces delays.
- Proactive Relief Measures: Disaster management teams and relief forces are positioned across key zones to coordinate emergency operations.
Context and Background
The southwest monsoon is driven by active atmospheric formations and upper-air cyclonic circulations converging over northern and central territories. These wind patterns trigger rainfall across a wide belt, raising water levels in rivers and saturating mountain soil. Civic authorities and emergency response teams have mobilized to prepare for potential landslips and drainage failures.
This weather highlights the importance of real-time forecasting and pre-emptive public alerts. Coordinating color-coded warnings across state borders helps disaster management agencies reduce travel-related incidents. For tourists, understanding these alerts is essential to navigate seasonal transitions safely and avoid hazardous road conditions.
Details of IMD Color-Coded Alerts and Heavy Monsoon Rainfall
The India Meteorological Department's color-coded system communicates the severity of incoming weather to the public and local administrations. The red alert in Himachal Pradesh signals extreme weather, requiring immediate protective action. In contrast, the yellow alert in Delhi advises travelers of potential disruptions to daily routines.
In mountainous areas, the impact of heavy monsoon rainfall is magnified by steep slopes and fragile terrain. Persistent downpours weaken soil structures, leading to rockfalls and blocked highways. In urban environments, sudden downpours can overwhelm drainage capacity, causing waterlogging.
To clarify the differences in alert levels and regional hazards, the following table summarizes the weather details:
| Regional Administrative Zone |
Current Alert Status |
Primary Weather Conditions |
Key Travel & Infrastructure Risks |
| Himachal Pradesh |
Red Alert (Extreme Danger) |
Persistent heavy downpours |
Landslides, flash floods, impassable passes |
| Delhi (National Capital) |
Yellow Alert (Be Aware) |

Localized waterlogging, traffic delays |
| Northern Mountain Passes |
High-Risk Warnings |
Rain convergence, low visibility |
Debris, rockfalls, blocked highways |
| Central Plain Territories |
Yellow Alert / Advisory |
Converging cyclonic circulations |
Low-lying flooding, transit disruptions |
Operational Risks and Severe Disruptions for Travelers
The monsoon systems present operational risks for commuters and tourists in northern India:
- Highway Blockages: Debris and rockfalls have rendered major highways in Himachal Pradesh impassable.
- Transit Congestion: Localized waterlogging in Delhi has led to delays across public and private transport networks.
- Visibility Hazards: Heavy cloud cover and mist in elevated regions restrict driving visibility along narrow roads.
- Utility Grid Issues: Water accumulation and winds can disrupt power grids, requiring urgent maintenance.
Economic Impact and Infrastructure Challenges from Heavy Monsoon Rainfall
Agriculture and hospitality in northern states are experiencing operational shifts due to seasonal weather. While agricultural communities rely on monsoon rains, excessive downpours result in crop damage and soil erosion. Additionally, hospitality and tourism in Himachal Pradesh face sudden booking cancellations as travelers postpone hill trips.
Furthermore, administrations face increased expenditure on emergency infrastructure repair. Keeping transit networks clear requires the constant mobilization of machinery to remove landslide debris. Restoring power grids and clearing flooded streets has placed additional pressure on municipal budgets.
What Authorities and Disaster Relief Organizations Are Saying
Disaster management units urge residents in high-risk zones to remain vigilant and avoid unnecessary travel. Emergency response teams are positioned near landslide-prone slopes to assist affected populations. Civic authorities in Delhi advise residents to avoid waterlogged streets and steer clear of vulnerable electrical installations.
Additionally, administrations highlight progress in forecasting models. Deploying advanced early warning systems allows authorities to issue alerts earlier, enabling relief forces to position resources before heavy rainfall begins—a significant shift from past reactive rescue efforts.

title: UPDATE: Cooling shelter extends operation in Medford amid extreme heat - Rogue Valley Times
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Chhattisgarh
full_text:
UPDATE: Cooling shelter extends operation in Medford amid extreme heat
Published 10:15 am Tuesday, July 21, 2026
The city of Medford and community partners are opening a cooling shelter downtown for an extra day due to extreme temperatures in the Rogue Valley.
The shelter, located at 332 W. Sixth St. and operated by community action agency ACCESS, is open until 8 p.m. Tuesday, July 21, and will be open from noon to 8 p.m. Wednesday, July 22, the city announced Tuesday evening.
The shelter will provide free snacks and cold drinks for anyone who visits, and pets are welcome. People may come and go as they please.
Temperatures in Medford are expected to rise as high as 101 degrees by late afternoon Tuesday and 103 on Wednesday, according to the National Weather Service.
For people seeking updates on the cooling shelter via text notification, text MED4SHELTERS to 888777.
To volunteer or contribute and help ACCESS, email volunteer@accesshelps.org or visit accesshelps.org.
